title: SEGMENT 5 — T-DM1 — Kadcyla and the maytansine approach

lines.text: SEGMENT 5 — T-DM1 — Kadcyla and the maytansine approach
In 2013, the FDA approved a drug called ado-trastuzumab emtansine, marketed as Kadcyla.
Among insiders it's almost always called T-DM1, which is a useful name to learn because it tells you exactly what the molecule is.
The T is trastuzumab — the famous anti-HER2 antibody.
The DM1 is the payload, a maytansine derivative.
And the dash in between is the linker.
T-DM1: trastuzumab plus DM1 plus a specific linker chemistry.
Read the name, get the molecule.
The target was HER2.

HER2 is a growth factor receptor that sits on the surface of many cells, but it is dramatically overexpressed on about fifteen to twenty percent of breast cancers — the so-called HER2-positive subtype.

Trastuzumab, the bare antibody, had been approved back in 1998 under the brand name Herceptin, and it had transformed HER2-positive breast cancer from one of the most aggressive forms of the disease to one of the most treatable.
Trastuzumab works through several mechanisms — it blocks HER2 signaling, it recruits immune cells to kill HER2-positive cells through a process called antibody-dependent cellular cytotoxicity.
But trastuzumab alone is not curative in metastatic disease.
Many patients respond initially, then progress.
The question was: could we make trastuzumab more powerful by hanging a payload on it?
The payload was DM1, which stands for maytansinoid 1.
Maytansine is a natural product originally isolated from an Ethiopian shrub called Maytenus serrata.
It was studied as a potential chemotherapy in the 1970s and 1980s and abandoned because it was too toxic to use as a free drug.
Like MMAE, maytansine works on microtubules — it binds tubulin and prevents spindle formation, causing mitotic arrest.
But maytansine binds at a different site on tubulin than the auristatins, and it has somewhat different cellular pharmacology.
DM1 is a modified version of maytansine that has been chemically engineered to allow attachment to a linker.
The linker chemistry for T-DM1 was a deliberate departure from the Adcetris approach.
Instead of a cleavable peptide linker, T-DM1 uses a non-cleavable thioether linker called SMCC.
The name doesn't matter — what matters is the concept.
The linker between the antibody and DM1 is a stable chemical bond that cannot be cleaved by any enzyme in the cell.
The only way to release the payload is to digest the entire antibody itself.
How does that work?
When T-DM1 binds to HER2 on a cancer cell, the whole complex gets internalized into the cell through receptor-mediated endocytosis.
The vesicle containing the complex fuses with a lysosome, which is the cellular trash compactor — a low-pH compartment packed with proteases and other digestive enzymes.
The lysosomal proteases chew up the antibody protein piece by piece, but they can't break the thioether linker.
What's released at the end of this process is not free DM1 — it's DM1 still attached to the single amino acid residue, lysine, that it had been conjugated to on the antibody.
So the active molecule that ends up free inside the cell is lysine-MCC-DM1, a small molecule consisting of DM1 plus the linker plus a single lysine.
This non-cleavable design has consequences.
First, it's even more stable in circulation than a cleavable linker — there's essentially no way for the payload to leak free in the bloodstream.
Second, the released payload, lysine-MCC-DM1, is a charged molecule, which is important because it has trouble crossing cell membranes.
Once it's released inside a target cell, it tends to stay inside that target cell.
It does its job locally — killing the HER2-positive cell it was delivered to — but it doesn't easily diffuse out to kill neighboring cells.
That's a feature in some ways.
It means T-DM1 has a relatively clean toxicity profile in tissues that don't express HER2.
But it's also a limitation, and the limitation matters.
In a tumor, HER2 expression is heterogeneous.
Some cells express HER2 at very high levels.
Some express it at moderate levels.
Some express it at low or undetectable levels.
With T-DM1, only the cells that express enough HER2 to be efficiently targeted will internalize the drug and be killed.
The neighboring cells with low or no HER2 expression get a free pass.
This is what oncologists call antigen-low escape, and it's one of the major mechanisms by which T-DM1 fails over time.
T-DM1 was approved in 2013 for HER2-positive metastatic breast cancer that had progressed on Herceptin and a taxane.
It worked — it extended progression-free survival meaningfully compared to other available options.
It became a standard second-line therapy for HER2-positive metastatic disease, and it has been used for years in that setting, generating about two billion dollars a year at peak.
It is a real drug, with real benefit, for real patients.
But it also has real limits.
The non-cleavable design and the charged payload mean that T-DM1 only kills the cells it directly targets.
It can't reach into a tumor and kill cells the antibody never bound.
And for patients with HER2-low disease — the much larger population whose tumors have some HER2 expression but not enough to be classified as HER2-positive — T-DM1 doesn't work at all.
There's just not enough antigen for the drug to do its job.
T-DM1 was the high-water mark of the first-generation ADC design philosophy.
Stable, clean, internalize-and-kill, no leakage.
It did its job for HER2-positive patients.
But it set the stage for the next drug, the one that would break the rules of first-generation ADC design and, in doing so, rewrite an entire chapter of breast cancer treatment.
That drug is T-DXd.
